reviews.review_msg:
He is a veteran of his industry. He is very passionate about certain issues and is very knowledgeable about the theory behind news and broadcasting. He also explains well, is reasonably balanced during discussions about controversial topics in class

In order to do well in papers, just quote+cite and format a bunch of journals that prove your point

I'm pretty sure this was the best class I ever took at SFU. Most of the times we want classes to be easy in order to consider them a great class but this class was challenging in a good way. I learned quite a lot and got out of my bubble and spoke in lectures. This is because he was super engaging and insightful.

Al-Rawi is honestly one of the best teachers I've had at SFU. If you are ever considering taking CMNS316 with him I'd highly encourage it. I am usually a pretty shy person, and his lectures often require student participation in the form of class discussion, but he makes it a really comfortable learning environment where anyone can speak up.
Great professor,  so organized and straight to the point

He's a nice guy but he was not able to teach the materials properly. It's very crucial because this is a 4 credit and Q course, so the fact that he rushes his lectures and makes us rely on the textbook makes it so much harder. Avoid him for this particular CMNS course. His quizzes are also worth so much, it's ridiculous.

Ahmad was a great professor, and he was super organized in his lectures, readings and any given assignments. The class was 4 credits, and was switched to online, but still he managed to get things ready for students. I would love to take another course with him in the future .

He was very nice and caring towards his students. The course was supposed to be in-person but got moved to online so there was no final exam. We had 3 main assignments, lab and tutorial participation, and weekly lecture reviews.
This seminar course was really enjoyable, from the lively discussion to the super relevant topics (current events), and Ahmed's teaching style and good spirit was definitely a part of that.

With the switch to the online medium, Ahmed has been accommodating and makes online lectures really enjoyable. Participation matters in this class but he fosters an open environment where even people who don't like to talk in class talk.

Ahmed was a great teacher for this course. I took this course during the early emergence of COVID-19 in the spring term of 2020. He was a great, understanding prof that always made sure our questions were answered. There is a fair amount of work for weekly assignments but as long as you are in lecture and pay attention, the course isn't difficult.

Ahmed is my favourite professor at SFU. He is so understanding and caring towards his students. He is also very passionate about what he teaches which makes all his courses very exciting. I took CMNS 201 with him as well and loved it! Best professor ever!

Ahmed tends to rush through his lecture slides and does not give reasonable time for us to write notes. I learn more from reading the textbook than listening to his lectures because he briefly skims over the concepts and does not go in depth at all. There are 4 quizzes and 1 final quiz and 3 assignments so the workload is quite heavy.

unorganized, would go through important topics as fast as he could. i didn't like his teaching method because it seemed like he was unfamiliar with the material. im sure he is knowledgeable however i found it extremely hard to be engaged in this class.

Worst Communications Prof i have ever had. There are 4 quizzes in total for this class AND a cumulative final 'Quiz' worth 20%. He zips right through lecture material so fast. He literally always says "Im going to go through this as fast as i can" all the time, so you cant take any efficient notes in preparation for the quiz's. Useless lectures.
